food_name: Teh O Siew Dai (Less Sweet Black Tea)
aliases: Black tea with less sugar no milk

english: Black tea with less sugar no milk

food_subgroup: Hot tea beverages, sweetened, no milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301103). No specific code exists for Singaporean-style less-sweet black tea without milk. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN000AJ covers black tea infusion generically. No specific code for Singaporean Teh O Siew Dai variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 represents tea as a substance. No specific code for Teh O Siew Dai. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of Teh O Siew Dai is approximately 250 ml, served hot in a ceramic cup or glass at a Singapore kopitiam or café.

nutrient_source.title: Teh O (Black Tea with Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central Tea Beverage Reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Teh O Siew Dai is a less-sweet variant of standard Teh O (black tea with sugar, no milk). HPB Foodo data for Teh O (black tea with sugar) was used as the primary reference and adjusted downward for sugar content to reflect the 'siew dai' (less sugar) preparation. Standard Teh O per HPB contains approximately 8–10 g sugar per 100 ml; siew dai reduces this by approximately 50%, yielding approximately 4–5 g sugar per 100 ml. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 14355, Beverages, tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water) was used to confirm baseline macro values for the tea infusion component.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Teh O Siew Dai specifically. GI is estimated at 30 based on the GI of lightly sweetened black tea analogues. Plain black tea has a GI of approximately 0 (no glycemic carbohydrates); the addition of a small amount of sugar (sucrose, GI ~65) at approximately 4 g per 100 ml dilutes the overall GI substantially. The composite GI of approximately 30 is consistent with lightly sweetened tea beverages reported in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. GL per 250 ml serving = (30 × 10 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~7.5 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Teh O Siew Dai is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content (~7.5 mg per serving); however, patients should be aware that caffeine in black tea may cause a transient blood pressure rise and should moderate total daily intake accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Reduced sugar content compared to standard Teh O; low glycemic load (~3.0 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Teh O Siew Dai is a lower-sugar alternative to standard Teh O, with approximately 10 g of carbohydrate per 250 ml serving and a glycemic load of approximately 3.0, making it acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation as part of a controlled carbohydrate diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, and low sugar; black tea flavonoids may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Teh O Siew Dai contains no fat or saturated fat and provides black tea polyphenols (theaflavins and thearubigins) which have been associated with modest improvements in endothelial function and LDL oxidation in peer-reviewed studies; the reduced sugar formulation further limits cardiovascular risk compared to fully sweetened variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh O Siew Dai (少甜無奶茶) is a popular Singaporean kopitiam beverage consisting of hot brewed black tea served without milk and with a reduced quantity of sugar compared to the standard Teh O preparation. The name derives from Hokkien: 'Teh' (tea), 'O' (black, i.e., without milk), and 'Siew Dai' (少甜, meaning less sweet). It is a staple of Singapore's hawker centre and café culture, typically prepared by brewing strong Ceylon or local-blend black tea through a cloth sock filter and adding a smaller-than-standard measure of condensed sugar syrup or granulated sugar. The resulting beverage is amber-coloured, mildly astringent, lightly sweet, and aromatic. Per 100 ml, Teh O Siew Dai provides approximately 16 kcal, 4 g of carbohydrate (primarily from the reduced sugar addition), negligible protein, and zero fat. It is dairy-free, vegan, and halal. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low GI) due to the diluted sugar content, with a glycemic load of approximately 3.0 per standard 250 ml cup. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease when consumed in moderation, as it is low in sodium, fat-free, and lower in sugar than standard sweetened tea preparations. Black tea polyphenols present in the beverage may offer modest antioxidant and cardioprotective benefits.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists specifically for Teh O Siew Dai as a distinct food code. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo Teh O (standard) data with a documented 50% sugar reduction to reflect the siew dai preparation, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central black tea baseline. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Cuisine corrected from 'Italian' (input error) to 'Singaporean' — Teh O Siew Dai is a quintessential Singapore kopitiam beverage with Hokkien-origin naming. 2. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero, as this is a black tea beverage with no milk or fat-containing ingredients. 3. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero by composition. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as tea infusion contains no dietary fibre. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as no animal-derived fat is present. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as no fortification or animal-derived components are present. 8. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for brewed black tea. 9. glycemic_index set to 30 (low GI) as an analogous estimate for lightly sweetened black tea; gi_category low per GI threshold rules (GI ≤ 55 = low). glycemic_load_per_serving = (30 × 10 g carbohydrate per 250 ml serving) / 100 = 3.0. 10. Fields set to -1 (not reported in approved sources): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — these micronutrients are not reported for brewed black tea in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central at the level of precision required. 11. protein_g is 0.1 g per 100 ml — trace amount consistent with USDA FoodData Central NDB 14355 (brewed black tea, 0.07 g/100 ml rounded to 0.1). Not zero. 12. Restaurant cuisine tags ('Italian', 'Pizza') in the input are artefacts of the field survey restaurant classification for The Light Cafe and do not reflect the food's actual cuisine origin.
